utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi frank how are you i heard the medical assistant told me that you're having some shortness of breath

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i am i am i am okay i am not great

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you are okay you are not great okay well how long have you been having some shortness of breath

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's been about a week

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it's been about a week okay now have you ever experienced these symptoms before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah this happened about six months ago and i came in and they changed my lasix dose and i seemed to get better

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so let's talk a little bit about that now i know that you have a history of coronary disease you know you had had a heart attack back in twenty eighteen we put that stent in your right coronary artery i know that the pumping function of your heart was a little low you know that what we call that ejection fraction was about thirty five percent have you been watching your salt intake

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah but you know it's been the holidays the last couple of weeks and you know i we've had a bunch of family get togethers and i i do n't think my diet has been everything it should have been

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright now have you noticed any swelling in your legs

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes definitely

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and are you taking the lasix now i know that we had increased it to about forty milligrams once a day are you taking it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i take it everyday

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you take it everyday okay do you notice that you're urinating any less recently

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no it seems about the same

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i i urinate at night you know at least once sometimes twice a night that's a little more it happened before

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright and do you feel short of breath at rest or is it mostly when you're just walking around

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: walking around stairs in particular walking quickly

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and how about when you're sleeping at night are you able to lay flat or do you have to sit up a little bit

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i gone from one pillow to two pillows

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright and do you ever wake up really short of breath does it ever wake you up

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it did that six months ago it has not done that so far this time

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright now tell me a little bit about when was the last time you saw your nephrologist

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: about about a month ago

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay because as you know you know we we do talk about the fact that you have some kidney dysfunction about stage three so it's gon na be important for you to continue to follow up with your kidney doctor now are you how are you doing staying away from things like motrin and advil i know that you had had some knee pain in the past and you know how are you doing staying away from those medications

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah everybody has told me not to use it so i do n't except when i have pretty bad pain i had an episode about a week ago some pain in my feet actually and i i had to take motrin to get rid of it so yeah i took motrin for a while and i stopped as quickly as i could

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay now are you able to take tylenol do you not feel any relief from tylenol when you take it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah tylenol's lovely it just is not very effective

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright yeah because i think we really want you to stay away from that those anti-inflammatories because that can make your kidney function worse and obviously you know we do n't wan na see you go on dialysis or anything like that so we'll have

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mean neither

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: right so we'll have to you know really work on that now tell me a little bit about are you watching the potassium and stuff in your diet

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nobody's really talked to me about that so

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay yeah so you want

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i did n't know what high potassium foods are

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay i'm sorry i've never talked to you about that in the past i that's my fault so yeah so you wan na make sure you stay away from things like bananas oranges you know potatoes have lots of potassium that type of thing and so you know because with your kidney function you know that can cause your potassium levels to go up because you do n't get rid of the potassium as you should you know

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: something to eat

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: what's that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: is there anything left is there anything left over that i ca n't eat

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so yeah you can eat you know how about this how about we give you a a referral to a dietitian and they can help you with that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sounds good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: now are you watching the nba playoff should i or should i not bring that up i is that a sensitive subject i mean i'm a self expand and and we did sweep the nuts so

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah well i mean that's fan and i could n't sleep last night but you know

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay well maybe we should n't talk about

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: kinda used to it you know where they where the favorite to win and then like that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah i know i i i will say i think jason tatum and jaylen brown paid played phenomenally so i do think that they help with the cell fix over the edge so but that's all we'll talk about that what's that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i said yeah too bad they did

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah alright well i wan na just go ahead and do a quick physical exam on you okay so you know looking at your vital signs here in the office you know your your blood pressure is about one thirty five over eighty so it's it's not too bad and maybe like to see it a little bit lower i'm just gon na be calling out my physical exam findings and i'm gon na let you know what they mean when i'm done okay so on your neck exam i appreciate some jugular venous distention up to about nine centimeters there are no carotid bruits on your lung exam i do appreciate bibasilar crackles with occasional rhonchi that clears when you cough on your heart exam i do appreciate a three out of six systolic ejection murmur heard at the left base and on your lower extremity exam i do appreciate one to two plus pitting edema bilaterally so frank what does that mean all that means is that you have findings on you that suggest that you're retaining fluid now we'll have to talk a little bit about how we're going to address that okay so i wan na just go over a little bit about my assessment and my plan for you okay so for the for your first problem of your shortness of breath i do believe you're having a an acute heart failure exacerbation so i wan na go ahead and increase your lasix from forty milligrams a day to eighty milligrams a day and i want you to call me in two days and let me know how your weight has been with increasing the diuretic now if you start to have worsening shortness of breath before that i want you to yeah call me we may have to admit you to the hospital for some intravenous diuretics okay i also wan na go ahead and just obtain

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: salt on lasix all at once am i take it all at once or do i divide it up

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how about you start taking the the increased dose in the morning and then we'll see how you do and if necessary i'll have to add a second dose in the afternoon that you can take around four o'clock in the afternoon i do n't want you to take it too late at night because then you will be going to the bathroom all night okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that sounds correct

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so i also want to go ahead and order a chest x-ray just to make sure that we are not missing anything else okay for your second problem of your coronary artery disease you know it sounds like you're doing well except for this heart failure exacerbation so i wan na go ahead and order another echocardiogram to check out that heart murmur that i hear on your physical exam just to make sure that that's not getting any worse and to make sure that the pumping function of your heart is stable and i'm gon na go ahead and call doctor white and let them know that i want you to be seen by them okay okay and then for your third problem of your stage three chronic kidney disease i want you to go ahead and try to you know do n't please do n't take anymore motrin or advil i do want you to try to stick with tylenol and if you're still having a significant problem with with some pain i want you to call me and we'll talk about some alternative medications that we can use or therapies okay i will also want to go ahead and order a complete metabolic panel just to make sure that your kidney function is stable does that sound okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that sounds good do you have any idea what was going on with my foot pain before

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well how long have you been having it how long you've been having that foot pain

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's why i took the motrin it lasted you know four five days slowly went away

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it was in both of your feet

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: one

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: one and where in your foot was was it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: just in front of the big toe

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: just in front of the big toe okay have you ever had a history of gout

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nobody has told me about that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright well it sounds like it could've been gout you know with your chronic kidneys chronic kidney disease it it's a possibility so for this possible gout flare for your fourth problem of your possible gout flare let's go ahead i wan na just go ahead and order a uric acid just to see and it's something that we can talk about there is an alternative medication that you can take with your kidney function that that would n't hurt it that would n't injure it any further and i think it would work just the same

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay any other questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: just as long as you think i'm gon na get better

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i think you're gon na get better yes i think we're gon na you know i really want you to watch your salt intake and i'm gon na go ahead and order that dietary dietitian referral so that you can get your diet under control but you really have to watch your salt intake i think that that's kind of what this heart failure exacerbation is about maybe a little dietary indiscretion

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay anything else

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah no that's good thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay you're welcome bye

detail_list.summary: The doctor introduces the patient, Frank, and notes the chief complaint of shortness of breath.
The doctor asks about the duration of the patient's shortness of breath.
The patient reports the shortness of breath has lasted for about one week.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced these symptoms before.
The patient reports a similar episode six months ago that improved after a Lasix dose adjustment.
Coronary artery disease
The patient has a history of myocardial infarction in 2018 with a stent in the right coronary artery and an ejection fraction of 35%.
The doctor asks if the patient has been watching his salt intake.
The patient admits to poor dietary choices due to holiday gatherings.
The doctor asks if the patient has noticed any leg swelling.
The patient confirms he has leg swelling.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking his prescribed 40mg daily dose of Lasix.
The patient confirms he takes Lasix every day.
The doctor asks if the patient has noticed any decrease in urination.
The patient denies changes in urination volume.
The patient reports nocturia occurring once or twice a night.
The doctor asks if the shortness of breath occurs at rest or with exertion.
The patient reports shortness of breath occurs with exertion, specifically walking and stairs.
The doctor asks if the patient can lie flat while sleeping.
The patient reports needing two pillows to sleep instead of one.
The doctor asks if the patient wakes up short of breath at night.
The patient denies waking up short of breath during this current episode.
The doctor asks when the patient last saw his nephrologist.
The patient saw his nephrologist about a month ago.
Chronic kidney disease
The doctor asks how the patient is doing with avoiding NSAIDs like Motrin and Advil.
The patient admits to taking Motrin for foot pain about a week ago despite knowing he should avoid it.
The doctor asks if the patient has tried Tylenol for pain relief.
The patient reports Tylenol is ineffective for his pain.
The doctor asks if the patient is monitoring his potassium intake.
The patient states he has not received education regarding potassium intake.
The doctor advises the patient to avoid high-potassium foods like bananas, oranges, and potatoes due to his kidney function.
The doctor provides a referral to a dietitian for dietary assistance.
The doctor notes blood pressure is 135/80 mmHg.
Physical exam findings include jugular venous distention to 9 cm, bibasilar crackles with rhonchi, 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, and 1-2+ pitting edema.
Heart failure exacerbation
The doctor increases Lasix to 80mg daily.
The doctor instructs the patient to call in two days regarding weight changes and warns of potential hospital admission for IV diuretics if symptoms worsen.
The patient asks if the Lasix dose should be taken all at once or divided.
The doctor instructs the patient to take the increased dose in the morning, with a potential second dose in the afternoon if needed.
The doctor orders a chest x-ray.
The doctor orders an echocardiogram and a referral to Dr. White for coronary artery disease management.
The doctor orders a complete metabolic panel for chronic kidney disease monitoring.
The patient asks about the cause of his previous foot pain.
The patient describes the foot pain as being in front of the big toe.
The doctor asks if the patient has a history of gout.
Gout flare
The doctor orders a uric acid test.

case_description: heart failure exacerbation; coronary artery disease;Stage 3 chronic kidney disease and possible gout flare
